EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frequent wind gusts from 50 to 70 mph caused numerous problems in southwest Indiana. The low pressure center that was the remnant of Hurricane Ike raced northeast at 40 to 50 mph across Missouri and Illinois. The center passed between Carbondale, IL and St. Louis, MO on its way toward the northeastern states. The rapid speed of the low pressure system contributed to this high wind event. A peak wind gust of 64 mph was measured by the automated observing system at the Evansville airport. The highest sustained wind at the airport was 47 mph. An unofficial wind gust of 66 mph was measured at Newburgh.||Extensive tree damage occurred. Trees were uprooted and very large limbs were broken. Some trees fell on homes and vehicles, causing considerable damage. Other trees fell across roads, blocking them. Major roads were re-opened by nightfall, though smaller streets were blocked for up to two days. Blocked roads in Pike County included the major state roads, including Highways 61, 57, and 64. Roof and siding damage was reported on numerous structures. In Posey County, the north wall of the Mount Vernon water plant blew apart. Large signs were blown down. Numerous power poles and utility lines were down. Complete power restoration took several days. The Red Cross opened  shelters in Evansville, Princeton, and Mount Vernon for those without power. In Winslow in Pike County, one person was trapped when a tree landed on a house. The person was rescued uninjured. In Evansville, a man was hospitalized after being trapped under a fallen tree for nearly an hour. Downed wires started a few field fires. A four-alarm field fire occurred near Newburgh in Warrick County. In Oakland City (Gibson County), an eight-square block area was evacuated for hours after a tree landed on a natural gas line. Railroad crossing gates were blown off in Chandler (Warrick County), and trees were cleared from railroad tracks. A sliding door was blown off a church near Evansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early morning hours tropical storm Ike was located near western Arkansas, while a slowly advancing cold front and tropical plume of moisture to the northwest helped steer Ike across Indiana during the afternoon hours. An upper level trough pushed through the great lakes overnight...pushing Ike and the cold front quickly to the northeast, with mid level drying moving in during the late evening hours.||This episode generally produced the heaviest rainfall in northwest Indiana and Illinois...rather than the originally expected location of central Indiana as it moved through. The speed of the system, increasing as it neared Indiana, helped keep precipitation amounts to a manageable level. Much of southern and eastern Indiana received little or no rain from Ike���s passage. Widespread wind damage occurred however. The peak wind speed at the Indianapolis airport was 63 mph, but areas in southeast Indiana experienced gusts approaching 80 mph. ||Because of wind damaged corn fields, many farmers bought equipment for their combines to pick up the downed corn. Harvesting of the corn fell behind schedule due to the necessity of slow combine speeds in damaged fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Weatherbug site at Seymour-Jackson Elementary School measured a 67 MPH wind. Time of this event is estimated.

The damage reports were described by the county sheriff's office as scattered in nature county-wide. A tree limb fell across West Sixth Street in Seymour just west of Emerson Drive around 12 PM EST, hitting a passing car (with no injuries). Another tree was across Elm Street between Fourth and Third streets. Vinyl siding was stripped off a house at Third and Poplar streets northeast of the American Legion Post in downtwon Seymour. Tree limbs littered Gaiser, Sheilds, and Kessler parks in Seymour. At least two or three trees were uprooted at Gaiser Park on the city's south side. Duke Energy reported 9,349 customers without power in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Ike moved across the Ohio Valley on the morning and afternoon of September 14th. This system combined with an upper level trough and surface cold front approaching the region to bring very strong surface wind gusts to the area. Widespread damage occurred, with measured wind gusts in excess of 81 mph.  Three deaths were directly attributed to the storm, along with 4 known injuries across south central Indiana, where more than 100,000 people were without power for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed at the Patoka Lake campground. Unknown structural damage was reported, along with trees and power lines down across the county. An anemometer on a hilltop location in Paoli measured an 81 mph gust.

An upper level trough pushed through the great lakes overnight...pushing Ike and the cold front quickly to the northeast, with mid level drying moving in during the late evening hours.||This episode generally produced the heaviest rainfall in northwest Indiana and Illinois...rather than the originally expected location of central Indiana as it moved through. The speed of the system, increasing as it neared Indiana, helped keep precipitation amounts to a manageable level. Much of southern and eastern Indiana received little or no rain from Ike���s passage. Widespread wind damage occurred however. The peak wind speed at the Indianapolis airport was 63 mph, but areas in southeast Indiana experienced gusts approaching 80 mph. ||Because of wind damaged corn fields, many farmers bought equipment for their combines to pick up the downed corn. Harvesting of the corn fell behind schedule due to the necessity of slow combine speeds in damaged fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Greensburg County sign was ripped from the outside wall of City Hall, which suffered minor damage. A tree was snapped in front of the building. The winds snapped trees and ripped down power lines with some shingles and siding torn and tossed about. The hardest hit area in the city was the airport. A hangar on the southside of the property as virtually torn apart. A small plane was seriously damaged. Pieces of the hangar flew off and destroyed the right wing. Duke Energy reported 4,130 customers without power in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Electrical service was interrupted in most of Jennings County starting late in the morning and escalating throughout the afternoon hours. Duke Energy reported 6,759 customers without power. Jennings County Dispatch became inundated with calls for downed power lines and limbs in roadways. At least one road, Deer Creek Road, was closed because of the number of power lines down, according to dispatch. Several other county roads were closed due to downed trees and limbs. In North Vernon, a large Pin oak tree fell on a large Coachman camper, a huge Beech tree fell near Middle School Road, Bradford pear trees fell along the city park's walkway, and a large tree fell on electrical lines in the Long Street area. A large uprooted tree came to rest against a two-story home at about a 45-degree angle at Jackson and Vernon streets in North Vernon, causing apparently minor damage. The winds were strong enough to break a large store front window at Greathouse Hardware in uptown North Vernon. Several windows upstairs in the building also were broken. A 1987 Ford Mustang was partially crushed underneath a fallen tree on County Road 500 South (Lovett Road) a short distance from Lovett. Time of incidents and cost estimates are very rough.

An upper level trough pushed through the great lakes overnight...pushing Ike and the cold front quickly to the northeast, with mid level drying moving in during the late evening hours.||This episode generally produced the heaviest rainfall in northwest Indiana and Illinois...rather than the originally expected location of central Indiana as it moved through. The speed of the system, increasing as it neared Indiana, helped keep precipitation amounts to a manageable level. Much of southern and eastern Indiana received little or no rain from Ike���s passage. Widespread wind damage occurred however. The peak wind speed at the Indianapolis airport was 63 mph, but areas in southeast Indiana experienced gusts approaching 80 mph. ||Because of wind damaged corn fields, many farmers bought equipment for their combines to pick up the downed corn. Harvesting of the corn fell behind schedule due to the necessity of slow combine speeds in damaged fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds blew down scattered trees and utility lines across the town and county as a result of gradient winds from the remnants of Ike.

Fallen power lines sparked a fire at a small garage at 26th Street and C Avenue in New Castle at about 1:20 PM EST. Firefighters prevented flames from damaging three cars parked inside. A tree limb fell on a truck and rear window of a parked car near A Avenue. Time of incident is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were measured at 59 MPH by a trained spotter 2 miles southeast of Brownsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front sparked a lengthy squall line that crossed all of southern Indiana on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups, one of which caused a fatality when a tree fell onto a mobile home near Henryville, Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees on several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused dangerous flash flooding and flooding across many locations in central Indiana.  A weak tornado also touched down in Greene County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm rains flooded numerous country roads across the county. Feather Creek began to flood at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front dropping south through the Great Lakes. 30 to 40 knots of shear as well as a moist unstable atmosphere allowed for several small bowing segments to develop with areas of wind damage reported.

Flooding occurred across central Indiana due to heavy rain.  Rain of five to more than eight inches fell on the 18th and 19th in much of southern Indiana near and south of U.S. Highway 50.  Many areas of southern Indiana were paralyzed for two or more days as a result.  Martin County declared an emergency and told drivers to stay off the roads until flood waters receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the White River in southern Knox County due to heavy rain.  Flooding continued until April 19th at 23:51.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July began on a dry note, but a persistent wet pattern prevailed from the 3rd through the 13th. Rain from the 3rd through the 9th kept ground conditions wetter than normal. Rainfall of 2 to nearly 6 inches on the 12th caused lowland river flooding to quickly return to portions of the White and Wabash Rivers in central Indiana.  The longest dry spell since March began on the 13th and continued through the 19th. This allowed rivers to return to near normal levels and central Indiana to dry out for the first time this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall created flooding in Greene county along the White River, including near Elliston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: July began on a dry note, but a persistent wet pattern prevailed from the 3rd through the 13th. Rain from the 3rd through the 9th kept ground conditions wetter than normal. Rainfall of 2 to nearly 6 inches on the 12th caused lowland river flooding to quickly return to portions of the White and Wabash Rivers in central Indiana.  The longest dry spell since March began on the 13th and continued through the 19th. This allowed rivers to return to near normal levels and central Indiana to dry out for the first time this year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall along the Wabash River caused flooding in Vermillion county, including near Montezuma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred across central Indiana due to heavy rain.  Rain of five to more than eight inches fell on the 18th and 19th in much of southern Indiana near and south of U.S. Highway 50.  Many areas of southern Indiana were paralyzed for two or more days as a result.  Martin County declared an emergency and told drivers to stay off the roads until flood waters receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Flatrock River in southeastern Shelby County due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowpack of one to three inches rapidly melted as warm air arrive in the region. This snowmelt, combined with a partially frozen, very moist ground and rainfall from two to locally over 3 inches, resulted in an increase in low land and river flooding running along and south of a Knox to north of Millersburg line. At the onset, some flash flooding occurred in areas experiencing rainfall rates of one-half to one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported eight to twelve inches of fast flowing water over roadways just east of Benton, around County Road 44 and 33.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall over parts of southeast Indiana. A tornado was also confirmed southwest of Aurora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five roads were flooded across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather and torrential downpours began to move through during the morning hours and continued into the early afternoon hours.  Flash flooding, damaging winds, and tornadoes all occurred with this system as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in the county due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front along with a strong upper level low produced a squall line that crossed south central Indiana. This set off widespread severe weather, and produced some heavy rains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather moved across central Indiana.  The main damage was caused by straight line winds, however an EF1 tornado also occurred in Greene County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A carport was blown on its side and metal was ripped from several homes in the Van Bibbler Lake area due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across southwest Indiana during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it approached southern Illinois, then swept east across southwest Indiana. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.

Severe thunderstorms developed across parts of central Illinois and west central Indiana during the evening hours of June 4th and then moved northeast across northwest Indiana through the early morning hours of June 5th.  Heavy rain and flash flooding was also reported.

During the early- to mid-evening hours, the Storm Prediction Center upgraded central, and much of southern, Indiana to a moderate risk for severe weather and issued a severe thunderstorm watch for the area effective until 3 AM EST. The atmosphere was extremely unstable and the growth of any developing storms was unimpeded. A roughly west to east frontal boundary stretched from southern Nebraska to central Indiana. Dewpoints in the lower 70s F were in place along the front, contributing to strong instability. A strong shortwave trough skirted the area, enhancing coverage of storms. Winds were expected to be the primary threat, and this is what indeed ending up being the main problem in central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, being blown down by thunderstorm winds, were responsible for downed power and phone lines. Downed trees also closed a number of city streets. One of the most extensively damaged areas was in the 1000 block of North Harrison Street.  Multiple trees were downed, with one falling on a residence. The tree caused extensive damage to the rear of the single-story residence. Two blocks north, a number of downed trees forced the closure of Park Boulevard from 13th Street to Main Street. The 500 and 600 blocks of Harrison Street were reduced to a single lane and eventually closed to any traffic. An 80-foot pine tree was uprooted in the 1200 block of Washington Street, struck a transformer and resulted in a 12-hour power outage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the middle and late afternoon hours of May 30th across east central Illinois and moved east across northwest Indiana during the evening hours.  These thunderstorms produced large hail, heavy rain and some flooding.

A powerful cold front moved rapidly southeast across southwest Indiana during the late afternoon hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms developed along the front as it approached southern Illinois, then swept east across southwest Indiana. Widespread damaging winds accompanied the line of storms. Temperatures fell about 30 degrees in less than one hour when the very strong cold front passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many homes around the county sustained some type of wind damage, mostly minor. At least two homes had roofs blown off, including one in Fort Branch and one near Oakland City. A church in Johnson was partially unroofed. A single wide mobile home just west of Johnson was overturned. A line of utility poles went down in Fort Branch, leaving the entire town without power. The ticket booth, concession stand, bleachers, and light towers were damaged at a high school. In Princeton, a tree fell across a main street and onto a vehicle. Near Oakland City, one house was destroyed. Several other houses and a barn had roof damage. A turkey farm received major damage, where over 2,000 turkeys, a brooder house, and a garage were destroyed. Indiana Route 57 was closed four miles north of the Indiana 64 junction because of downed power lines. About 15 people spent the night at a Red Cross shelter in Fort Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through central Indiana during the evening hours of January 29th.  Hail, damaging winds, and a tornado occurred with this storm system in central Indiana.  The tornado was rated EF1 and occurred in Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal building was blown down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through central Indiana during the evening hours of January 29th.  Hail, damaging winds, and a tornado occurred with this storm system in central Indiana.  The tornado was rated EF1 and occurred in Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front sparked a lengthy squall line that crossed all of southern Indiana on the evening of January 29. A large number of locations had 60 to as much as 100 mph winds, causing extensive property damage.  There were also a few small tornado spin-ups, one of which caused a fatality when a tree fell onto a mobile home near Henryville, Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half-mile wide swath of damaging straight-line winds stretched for four miles from Henryville to the Scott County line, with wind gusts to near 90 mph. Numerous trees were snapped or uprooted by down burst winds on Beagle Club Road and Old State Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding struck central Indiana in February.  Some of the same areas heavily flooded during January were once again flooded to near record levels.  February flooding was more widespread and persisted longer than flooding during January.  One person was killed because he drove into  a flooded road.  Snowfall of up to 10 inches accumulated during the early days of February in northern Indiana and portions of east central Illinois.  This snow quickly melted by the morning of the 6th as temperatures rose into the 50s on the 4th and 5th.  Rain of 1 to 4 inches fell in eastern Illinois and much of Indiana on the 5th and 6th.  This combination of rapid snow melt and heavy rain caused extensive flooding of streams and rivers in central Indiana.  The heaviest rainfall was in the east central Illinois, northern Indiana, and eastern Indiana areas.  For the second time in 30 days, flooding approached record levels along the Tippecanoe River in northern Indiana.    The Wabash River approached a major flood from Lafayette to Vincennes.  Flooding along this portion of the Wabash River was the highest since January 2005.  Extensive flooding along the White, Eel, Muscatatuck, and East Fork White Rivers was the highest to date of the winter.  Flood damage along the Tippecanoe River was limited because many residents had not been able to repair January flood damage.  However, some flood damage did result to those who had begun repairs.  The number of people evacuated was much less as well because many homes were uninhabitable.  An agricultural levee south of Terre Haute was over toppled.  A few rural families had roads to their homes blocked by flood waters.  Sandbagging was necessary in the Montezuma and Clinton areas.  One person was killed during the evening of the 6th in Lawrence County.  He drove his vehicle into a road flooded by Indian Creek.  Numerous local roads and several state highways were flooded along Indiana's major rivers.  Flood lasted from a few days in many central Indiana locations to nearly four weeks along portions of the Wabash River in the Hutsonville area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Big Walnut Creek in Putnam County, including near Reelsville, due to heavy rain and snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began during late March continued into April.  Flooding occurred across central Indiana due to heavy rainfall. Just as the crest was reaching the Hazleton and Mount Carmel areas on the 27th, rain of one to nearly six inches fell from the evening of the 26th through the night of the 31st in southern Indiana. This additional rain prolonged the extensive flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the White River in Greene County, including near Newberry, due to heavy rain.  This flooding is a continuation from flooding that began on March 27th at 0730.  Flood damage estimates from March are near twelve thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred across central Indiana due to heavy rain.  Rain of five to more than eight inches fell on the 18th and 19th in much of southern Indiana near and south of U.S. Highway 50.  Many areas of southern Indiana were paralyzed for two or more days as a result.  Martin County declared an emergency and told drivers to stay off the roads until flood waters receded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in many locations throughout Monroe County.  Heavy rain caused high water which closed several roads.  One man was rescued by boat from the roof of a submerged vehicle.  One household was submerged by the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary over the lower Ohio Valley brought training thunderstorms and heavy rain to south central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occupants of a vehicle stalled in high water on Cox's Ferry Road were rescued. U.S. Highway 150 was closed near Fredericksburg. Several other roads around the county were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in the afternoon along a northward moving warm front.  Additional thunderstorms developed during the evening as the front moved slowly north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Wabash River occurred. Several cold fronts moved across the region through mid-month. Each front brought scattered storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Wabash River occurred. At the New Harmony gage, the river crested at 16.93 feet on the 20th. Flood stage there is 15 feet. Minor flooding of low bottomlands occurred, shutting down some oil wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed across central Illinois and southwest Indiana during the late afternoon and early evening hours along the southern-most of two low-level confluence features. A severe thunderstorm watch was issued at 5:05 PM EST due to further expected intensification of the storms as a result of strong daytime heating, moist air, increasing deep wind shear, and upper-level support. Over the next several hours new storms formed further north and east over Indiana, though remaining scattered in coverage and marginally severe. A new severe thunderstorm watch was issued at 9:15 PM EST to cover the continued development of storms, which were now ahead of an amplifying trough of low pressure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several structures were damaged on a farm as a result of thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure moved into northwestern Wisconsin with a strong cold front extending from it into the southern Great Lakes. Abundant moisture and a strong wind field set the state for a line of strong to severe storms to move into northwest parts of Indiana during the late evening hours of April 25th into the early morning hours of the 26th. While most activity remained below severe limits, a small cluster of storms was able to tap the strong wind fields and cause damaging winds in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported a large 100 year old tree being blown down by thunderstorm winds, destroying the second story of a residence. Damage is estimated at $50,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding that began during late March continued into April.  Flooding occurred across central Indiana due to heavy rainfall. Just as the crest was reaching the Hazleton and Mount Carmel areas on the 27th, rain of one to nearly six inches fell from the evening of the 26th through the night of the 31st in southern Indiana. This additional rain prolonged the extensive flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Wabash River in Tippecanoe County, including near Lafayette, due to heavy rain.  This flooding is a continuation from flooding that began on March 28th at 1630.  Flood damage estimates from March are around twelve thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of low pressure moved along a stationary front located through the Ohio Valley.  These waves of low pressure brought an extended period of heavy rain to the region, with rainfall totals between four and seven inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded.  Eight roads across the southern and eastern portions of the county were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Massive and historic flash flooding struck central Indiana.  The Great Flood of June 2008 was one of Indiana's costliest natural disasters.  Major roads and interstates flooded.  Heavy rainfall on May 30th allowed streams and rivers to rise.  Additional heavy rainfall on the 3rd and the 4th added to these high levels.  Severe weather brought heavy rainfall on the 6th as well.  The stage was set for historic flooding in portions of central and southern Indiana.  The storms on the 6th exacerbated the situation by laying down a boundary in southern Illinois and southern Indiana.  A moist southerly low level jet rode over this boundary for more than 12 hours continually generating showers and thunderstorms.  Rainfall of two to nearly eleven inches fell across portions of central and southern Indiana.  The impact of the rain was immediate with widespread flash flooding occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spencer had multiple flood damaged homes.  State Roads 43, 46, and 67 were all closed.  United States 231 was also closed.  Roads and bridges were washed out.  Water was over railroad tracks near Gosport.  Cars and golf carts were under water.  Over 60 roads were washed out.  Campers were stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through central Indiana during the evening hours of January 29th.  Hail, damaging winds, and a tornado occurred with this storm system in central Indiana.  The tornado was rated EF1 and occurred in Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a house due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable, sheared environment was in place across northern Indiana ahead of a cold front. The atmosphere remained capped until the late afternoon and evening hours, when storms began to develop rapidly. Several storms south of a Monon, Indiana to Decatur, Indiana line became strong and even in some instances severe. A few storms exhibited rotation with one tornado confirmed in Wabash County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown was confirmed in an open field just west of a residence on South State Road 13 south of West 400 S. Several trees were damaged with a pole barn being partially destroyed. A second barn had the north wall blown towards the north and a partial roof collapse as a result. Approximately 12 power poles were snapped or pushed over along State Route 13. The damage in the area was consistent with a EF1 tornado with maximum winds estimated at around 90 mph. Two minor injuries as two people were in the barn at the time the tornado struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through central Indiana during the evening hours of January 29th.  Hail, damaging winds, and a tornado occurred with this storm system in central Indiana.  The tornado was rated EF1 and occurred in Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust was measured with wind equipment mounted on a truck.  Numerous trees were also reported down around the city due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front across mainly northeastern Indiana. The storms interacted with 30 to 40 knots of shear and CAPES in excess of 2000 j/kg, resulting in several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through through the area earlier in the day allowing for warm temperatures over central Indiana.  Severe storms formed in advance of a cold front that moved through later at night.  These storms contained hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.  Three tornadoes also formed with these storms including one that moved through the east side of Indianapolis.  Heavy rain caused flash flooding across many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Substantial tree damage occurred due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through through the area earlier in the day allowing for warm temperatures over central Indiana.  Severe storms formed in advance of a cold front that moved through later at night.  These storms contained hail and damaging thunderstorm winds.  Three tornadoes also formed with these storms including one that moved through the east side of Indianapolis.  Heavy rain caused flash flooding across many locations.
Flooding from December continued into the early portions of January along the Wabash River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Wabash River in Warren County due to heavy rain.  Flooding began on December 29th and continued into January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over east central Illinois and moved east across northwest Indiana during the late afternoon hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms produced heavy rainfall and caused flash flooding as they moved across central Indiana during the afternoon hours and through the overnight time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three trees were blown down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved across central Indiana bringing damaging winds, large hail, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Traffic was reduced to one lane on SR 67 by flood waters over roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system moved across central Indiana bringing damaging winds, large hail, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three utility poles that crossed 19th Street from Ohio Boulevard to Poplar Street were blown down. One of the poles cracked and fell on a car with a woman inside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and an upper level trough set off some severe thunderstorms and produced some heavy rains over parts of south central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 200W near Sunset Drive was impassable due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating and humid air destabilized the atmosphere during the late morning and early afternoon hours. Turbulent clouds developed along a west to east low-level confluence boundary and were aided by wind shear of 30 to 35 knots at low- to mid-levels of the atmosphere. These conditions increased the number and intensity of thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening. They produced damaging winds across Indiana from west to east just south of Indianapolis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trees and utility lines down.

A family of six was rescued from the flooded East Fork White River near County Road 150 East.  The family was taken to the medical center in Seymour for medical treatment after the incident.
